items.myth: A high silhouette score proves the clusters are correct for the business problem.
items.reality: Silhouette measures geometric cohesion and separation in feature space only; segments can score well yet be non-actionable or omit strategically important groups.
items.example: Silhouette 0.55 on demographic features may split customers by age bands that marketing cannot target differently.

items.myth: Inertia and silhouette always agree on the best k.
items.reality: Inertia rewards tightness monotonically; silhouette penalizes overlap and rewards separation—they can peak at different k values.
items.example: Inertia keeps dropping from k=4 to k=8 while silhouette peaks at k=5 then falls as small clusters fragment.

items.myth: External metrics like ARI are the same as classification precision and recall.
items.reality: ARI scores partition agreement between two labelings; precision/recall score predicted class labels from a supervised model. Use each in its proper task.
items.example: ARI = 0.8 between clusters and disease subtypes does not tell you recall on predicting new patients—that needs a classifier and PR-F1 metrics.

items.myth: If clusters look good visually in 2D, formal metrics are unnecessary.
items.reality: 2D projections distort high-dimensional geometry; quantitative metrics on the same scaled space used for clustering catch weak or unstable groups.
items.example: PCA scatter shows three blobs but silhouette on full 50-D features is 0.12 because separation exists only in projected axes.
